topics.title: What is Programming?
topics.content:
Programming is the process of creating a set of instructions that tell a computer how to perform a task. Programming can be done using many different languages such as Python, Java, C++, and more. At its core, programming involves problem-solving and logical thinking.

Key Concepts:
• Variables – containers for storing data values
• Data Types – integers, strings, booleans, floats
• Control Flow – if/else statements and loops
• Functions – reusable blocks of code

Programming is the foundation of all software, from mobile apps to operating systems.

topics.title: Control Structures
topics.content:
Control structures determine the flow of execution in a program. They allow you to make decisions and repeat actions.

1. Conditional Statements (if/else):
These let your program make decisions based on conditions. For example, checking if a user is old enough to vote.

2. Loops (for, while):
Loops repeat a block of code multiple times. A 'for' loop runs a set number of times, while a 'while' loop runs as long as a condition is true.

3. Switch/Case:
An alternative to long if/else chains when comparing a single variable against multiple values.

Mastering control structures is essential for writing efficient and logical programs.
topics.quiz_subject: control structures if else loops for while switch

topics.title: Functions and Modular Code
topics.content:
Functions are self-contained blocks of code that perform a specific task. They are the building blocks of modular programming.

Why use functions?
• Reusability – write once, use many times
• Readability – break complex problems into smaller pieces
• Testing – easier to test individual components
• Maintainability – changes in one place affect everywhere

Anatomy of a function:
1. Name – descriptive identifier
2. Parameters – input values
3. Body – the code that runs
4. Return value – output of the function

Good functions do one thing and do it well.
topics.quiz_subject: functions parameters return values modular programming

topics.title: Data Structures Basics
topics.content:
Data structures are ways of organizing and storing data so that they can be accessed and modified efficiently.

Common data structures:
• Arrays – fixed-size, ordered collections
• Lists – dynamic-size ordered collections
• Stacks – Last In, First Out (LIFO)
• Queues – First In, First Out (FIFO)
• Dictionaries/Maps – key-value pairs
• Trees – hierarchical data organization
• Graphs – networks of connected nodes

Choosing the right data structure can make the difference between an algorithm that runs in seconds vs hours.
topics.quiz_subject: data structures arrays lists stacks queues trees

topics.title: Algorithm Complexity
topics.content:
Big O notation is used to describe the performance or complexity of an algorithm.

Common Complexities:
• O(1) – Constant time
• O(log n) – Logarithmic time
• O(n) – Linear time
• O(n log n) – Linearithmic time
• O(n²) – Quadratic time

Understanding complexity helps you choose the most efficient solution for large data sets.
topics.quiz_subject: big o notation algorithm complexity time space

topics.title: Sorting Algorithms
topics.content:
Sorting is the process of arranging data in a specific order (ascending or descending).

Algorithms:
• Bubble Sort (O(n²))
• Selection Sort (O(n²))
• Insertion Sort (O(n²))
• Merge Sort (O(n log n))
• Quick Sort (O(n log n))

Efficient sorting is fundamental to search and data processing tasks.
topics.quiz_subject: sorting bubble merge quick insertion selection

resources.description: Access latest research papers and datasets.

resources.title: 🧪 Jupiter Labs
resources.description: Interactive notebook environments for ML experiments.

topics.title: What is Machine Learning?
topics.content:
Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of Artificial Intelligence that gives computers the ability to learn from data without being explicitly programmed.

Three types of ML:
1. Supervised Learning – training with labeled data (e.g., spam detection)
2. Unsupervised Learning – finding patterns in unlabeled data (e.g., clustering customers)
3. Reinforcement Learning – learning through trial and reward (e.g., game-playing AI)

ML powers recommendation engines, self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, and much more.

topics.title: Linear Regression
topics.content:
Linear Regression is one of the simplest ML algorithms. It models the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables by fitting a straight line.

The equation: y = mx + b
• y = predicted value
• m = slope (weight)
• x = input feature
• b = intercept (bias)

The goal is to find the best m and b that minimize the error between predictions and actual values. This is done using a method called Gradient Descent.

Linear Regression is used in sales forecasting, risk assessment, and trend analysis.
topics.quiz_subject: linear regression gradient descent slope intercept

topics.title: Classification Algorithms
topics.content:
Classification is a supervised learning task where the goal is to predict a categorical label.

Popular algorithms:
• Logistic Regression – despite the name, it is used for classification using a sigmoid function
• Decision Trees – tree-like model of decisions with if/then rules
• Random Forest – ensemble of many decision trees
• K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) – classifies based on the k closest data points
• Support Vector Machines (SVM) – finds the optimal hyperplane separating classes

Evaluation metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Confusion Matrix.
topics.quiz_subject: classification logistic regression decision trees KNN SVM

topics.title: Neural Networks Introduction
topics.content:
Neural Networks are computing systems inspired by biological neural networks in the brain.

Architecture:
• Input Layer – receives the raw data
• Hidden Layers – perform computations and learn features
• Output Layer – produces the prediction

Key concepts:
• Weights and Biases – learnable parameters
• Activation Functions – ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh
• Backpropagation – algorithm to update weights
• Loss Function – measures prediction error
• Epochs – number of training iterations

Deep Learning uses neural networks with many hidden layers to solve complex problems like image recognition and language translation.
topics.quiz_subject: neural networks layers activation backpropagation deep learning

topics.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
topics.content:
Sir Isaac Newton's three laws form the foundation of classical mechanics.

1st Law (Inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by an external force.

2nd Law (F=ma): The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force and inversely proportional to its mass.

3rd Law (Action-Reaction): For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

These laws explain everything from why a ball rolls to how rockets launch into space.
topics.quiz_subject: Newton laws of motion inertia force mass acceleration

topics.title: Energy and Work
topics.content:
Energy is the capacity to do work. Work is done when a force causes displacement.

Work = Force × Distance × cos(θ)

Types of energy:
• Kinetic Energy (KE) = ½mv² – energy of motion
• Potential Energy (PE) = mgh – stored energy due to position
• Thermal Energy – heat from molecular motion

Conservation of Energy: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed from one form to another.

Power = Work / Time (measured in Watts)
topics.quiz_subject: energy work kinetic potential conservation power

topics.title: Momentum and Collisions
topics.content:
Momentum is the product of an object's mass and velocity: p = mv

Conservation of Momentum: In an isolated system, total momentum before a collision equals total momentum after.

Types of collisions:
• Elastic – both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved (e.g., billiard balls)
• Inelastic – momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not (e.g., car crashes)
• Perfectly Inelastic – objects stick together after collision

Impulse = Force × Time = Change in Momentum (Δp)
topics.quiz_subject: momentum collisions elastic inelastic impulse conservation

topics.title: Carbon and Bonding
topics.content:
Organic chemistry is the study of carbon-containing compounds. Carbon is unique because it can form four covalent bonds, creating an enormous variety of molecules.

Types of bonds:
• Single bond (σ) – sharing of 1 pair of electrons
• Double bond (1σ + 1π) – sharing of 2 pairs
• Triple bond (1σ + 2π) – sharing of 3 pairs

Hybridization:
• sp3 – tetrahedral geometry (109.5°)
• sp2 – trigonal planar (120°)
• sp – linear (180°)

Carbon's versatility is why life on Earth is carbon-based.
topics.quiz_subject: organic chemistry carbon bonding hybridization sp3 sp2

topics.title: Functional Groups
topics.content:
Functional groups are specific groupings of atoms within molecules that determine the chemical properties.

Key functional groups:
• Hydroxyl (-OH) – Alcohols
• Carbonyl (C=O) – Aldehydes and Ketones
• Carboxyl (-COOH) – Carboxylic Acids
• Amino (-NH2) – Amines
• Phosphate (-PO4) – important in DNA/ATP
• Sulfhydryl (-SH) – Thiols

Recognizing functional groups helps predict how a molecule will react and what properties it will have.
topics.quiz_subject: functional groups hydroxyl carbonyl carboxyl amino organic

topics.title: The Microbial World
topics.content:
Microbiology is the study of organisms too small to see with the naked eye: bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists.

Key domains:
• Bacteria – prokaryotic, unicellular, diverse metabolisms
• Archaea – prokaryotic but genetically distinct from bacteria
• Eukarya – includes fungi, protists, and some algae

Viruses are acellular but studied in microbiology. They require a host cell to replicate.

Microbes are essential for nutrient cycling, food production, medicine, and biotechnology.
topics.quiz_subject: microbiology bacteria viruses fungi prokaryotic eukaryotic

topics.title: Bacterial Structure and Growth
topics.content:
Bacteria are single-celled prokaryotes with unique structures.

Key structures:
• Cell wall – provides shape and protection
• Plasma membrane – controls what enters/exits
• Ribosomes – protein synthesis
• Flagella – movement
• Pili – attachment and DNA transfer
• Capsule – protection from immune system

Bacterial growth phases:
1. Lag Phase – adaptation
2. Log Phase – exponential growth
3. Stationary Phase – growth = death
4. Death Phase – decline

Generation time can be as short as 20 minutes.
topics.quiz_subject: bacterial structure cell wall flagella growth phases lag log
